coredata.dc:title: Blending polydopamine-derived imprinted polymers with rice straw-based fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection and adsorptive removal of ibuprofen
coredata.dc:creator: Mohiuddin, Irshad
Singh, Raghubir
Kaur, Varinder
coredata.dc:description: Dual-functioning probes capable of detecting and removing hazardous substances have recently received increased attention compared to exclusive sensory probes. Herein, a new composite is synthesized by blending polydopamine imprinted polymers with fluorescent carbon dots (PIP-FCDs) for the selective recognition and adsorption of Ibuprofen (IBF). IBF is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug and is excessively released in the pharmaceutical wastes. The PIP-FCDs consist of confined pockets for encasing IBF and quenches fluorescence signal when contact with the molecule. PIP-FCDs show high sensitivity (limit of detection = 1.58 × 10−5 μM) and selectivity towards IBF in the presence of other pharmaceutical drugs i.e., aspirin, ketoprofen, norfloxacin, and levofloxacin. The adsorption studies show an adsorption capacity of 209.8 mg g−1 with an extraction efficiency of around 99.9 %. Furthermore, PIP-FCDs are utilized to determine IBF levels in various aqueous pharmaceutical samples. This development provides a simple and dual-functioning probe for the detection and adsorption of IBF from various matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ibuprofen
Dopamine
Fluorescent carbon dots
Molecularly imprinting polymers
Adsorption

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of polydopamine imprinted fluorescent carbon dots (PIP-FCDs) and polydopamine non-imprinted carbon dots (PNIP-FCDs)
Fluorescence studies and real samples analysis
Design and synthetic platform construction of the PIP-FCDs
Optimization of the adsorption process variables for enhancing extraction efficiency
Recognition performance and sensitivity of PIP-FCDs sensor
Utilization of the PIP-FCDs in practice

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge UGC-D. S. Kothari Post-Doctoral Fellowship scheme (No. F.4-2/2006 (BSR)/CH/20-21/0257) for providing financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical characterization results of the analyzed materials: Panel (a) FTIR spectrums, Panel (b-c) Nitrogen adsorption−desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size distributions, and Panel (d-e) TGA profiles.
XPS spectra of the PIP-FCDs: Panel (a) Full XPS survey; and high-resolution XPS spectra of Panel (b) C 1 s, Panel (c) N 1 s, and Panel (d) O 1 s.

(i) Morphological analysis of the synthesized materials by HR-TEM: Panel (a and b) spherical shape of FCDs (diameter of 4–9 nm), Panel (c and d) chain-like nodular structure of PIP-FCDs (shell thickness of 33–45 nm), Panel (e and f) rough polymeric surface of PNIP-FCDs (diameter of 70–100nm), (ii) Panel (g – i) SAED patterns of FCDs, PIP-FCDs, and PNIP-FCDs, displaying diffused rings reflecting the amorphous nature, and (iii) EDS elemental mapping images of Panel (j) FCDs, Panel (k) PIP-FCDs and Panel (l) PNIP-FCDs (C: carbon; N: nitrogen; O: oxygen).

Effect of optimizing parameters: Panel (a) Concentration, Panel (b) contact time (min), Panel (c) adsorbent amount (mg), Panel (d) temperature (K), Panel (e) pH, and Panel (f) zeta potential distribution graph. Error bars specify the RSD% of triplicate experiments.

Panel (a) 3D fluorescence spectrum of the PIP-FCDs showing the strongest emission peak, Panel (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized fluorescent materials: FCDs, PIP-FCDs with IBF, PIP-FCDs without IBF, and PNIP-FCDs, Panel (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of IBF and PIP-FCDs, Panel (d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of IBF and PIP-FCDs, Panel (e) Average emission response of PIP-FCDs at room temperature with time (upto 100 min), and Panel (f) Stability of PIP-FCDs probe evaluated by storage at 4 °C for one month. (Excitation wavelength = 415 nm; emission wavelength = 510 nm; Error bars specify triplicate measurements).

Fluorescence emission spectra of fluorescent probe with various concentrations of IBF from 0 to 200 μL: Panel (a) PIP-FCDs, Panel (b) PNIP-FCDs (Excitation wavelength: 415 nm; emission wavelength: 510 nm, Panel (c) Non-linear Stern–Volmer plot for IBF, and Panel (d) Fluorescence intensity as a function of concentration for the determination of LOD for IBF.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure for poly-dopamine imprinted fluorescent carbon dots.
Non-linear isotherm fitting parameters for the adsorption of IBF onto PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs.
Non-linear kinetic fitting parameters for the adsorption of IBF onto PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs.
Selective adsorption experimental results for PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs towards target IBF and competitive analogues.
Quantification of IBF in sewage water, tap water, and pharmaceutical samples by the proposed PIP-FCDs sensor.

head.ce:title: Blending polydopamine-derived imprinted polymers with rice straw-based fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection and adsorptive removal of ibuprofen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Sector-14, Chandigarh 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Sector-14, Chandigarh-160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector, 10, Chandigarh, -160011, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector 10, Chandigarh-160011, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-functioning probes capable of detecting and removing hazardous substances have recently received increased attention compared to exclusive sensory probes. Herein, a new composite is synthesized by blending polydopamine imprinted polymers with fluorescent carbon dots (PIP-FCDs) for the selective recognition and adsorption of Ibuprofen (IBF). IBF is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug and is excessively released in the pharmaceutical wastes. The PIP-FCDs consist of confined pockets for encasing IBF and quenches fluorescence signal when contact with the molecule. PIP-FCDs show high sensitivity (limit of detection = 1.58 × 10−5 μM) and selectivity towards IBF in the presence of other pharmaceutical drugs i.e., aspirin, ketoprofen, norfloxacin, and levofloxacin. The adsorption studies show an adsorption capacity of 209.8 mg g−1 with an extraction efficiency of around 99.9 %. Furthermore, PIP-FCDs are utilized to determine IBF levels in various aqueous pharmaceutical samples. This development provides a simple and dual-functioning probe for the detection and adsorption of IBF from various matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Health organizations and people are both extremely concerned about the continued presence of trace levels of new pollutants, particularly pharmaceutical residues, in the water. These days, ibuprofen (IBF) is excessively used as a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug for the treatment of pain, inflammation, and fever [1,2]. Therefore, its release as pharmaceutical residue in water bodies is increasing daily due to improper disposal. There is sufficient evidence that IBF may pose an increased risk to aquatic and terrestrial organisms due to their accumulation in the environment [3,4]. Therefore, developing an efficient system for selective detection and complete remediation of IBF is desirable.

The molecular imprinting technique is featured to produce analyte-selective probes by introducing confined cavities in synthetic polymers. The materials generated by imprinting can be described vividly as molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with “artificial locks” derived from “artificial keys.” These are considered better than natural receptors because of their physicochemical stability, resilience, great adaptability, and the affordable cost of manufacture [5]. These properties, along with selectivity and sensitivity, make their wide usage in the development of synthetic antibodies, biological or chemical material sensing, drug delivery, separation science, catalytic reactions, and degradation processes [6–10]. Furthermore, their blends with fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) have attracted more popularity to attain unique optical properties and excellent biocompatibility [11]. FCDs are water soluble, chemically inert, less toxic, biocompatible, photoluminescent, and easy to functionalize [12]. Due to the harmfulness and photo-bleaching apprehensions from semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, FCDs derived from rice are considered as superior fluorophores. In addition, their high photostability and unblinking photoluminescence, as compared to organic fluorophores, gave them an upper edge. Furthermore, using rice straw as a precursor material for synthesizing FCDs materials will create value and reduce environmental problems. Surface modification of rice straw-based FCDs with rich functional groups may enrich their selectivity and biocompatibility towards a specific chemical analyte type. The most common method used for the functionalization of FCDs to generate imprints involves sol-gel polymerization utilizing functional monomers (3-(aminopropyl)triethoxysilane) and cross-linker (tetraethyl orthosilicate). The primary drawbacks of the sol-gel method are challenges in regulating the size of particles, morphological characteristics, monodispersity, time-consuming, and very few silica precursors are accessible. The adsorption capacities of the recently developed IBF imprinted materials are reported in the range of 3.598 mg g−1–205.9 mg g−1 [13–18]. Several groups have recently reported the spectrofluorimetric sensing probes (limit of detection in μM is given in brackettes) including carbon paste electrode clay (3.2 × 10−2), CdTe quantum dots (1.6 × 10−5), glassy carbon electrode/multiwalled carbon nanotubes/ionic liquid/chitosan (2.0 × 10−5), carbon paste/multiwalled carbon nanotubes (9.1), conducting molecularly imprinted/quartz crystal microbalance (5.0 × 104), hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide surfactant-ZSM-5/Fe2O3 (1.5 × 10−2), cotton fibre@UiO-66-NH2 (1.6 × 10−2), multi-template molecularly imprinted polymer (4.8 × 10−3 and 1.8 × 10−3–4.9 × 10−3), sol–gel poly(tetrahydrofuran) (6.8 × 10−2), TiO2@Fe3O4@C-nanofibers (4.6 × 10−3), MoS2@Fe3O4@C-dot (5.3 × 10−5), and tri-color ratiometric fluorescent imprinted sensor (1.5 × 10−5) for IBF analysis [19–31]. Most of these sensors and adsorbent materials employed use of organic precursors however, their application is challenging in terms of subsequent handling or disposal due to the environmental concerns. Hence, developing sensors or adsorbent materials with good performance (e.g., high extraction efficiency) and environmental sustainability (e.g., biocompatible precursors) is important. Moreover, these aforementioned sensors suffered shortcomings such as low sensitivity, low recovery, and extended response time. Therefore, developing selective detection and adsorptive removal of IBF involving bio-based materials in various environmental compartments is the need of the hour.

Herein, a polydopamine (PDA) based imprinted network is generated around the FCDs to create confined cavities for IBF. PDA is a polymer of the natural biomolecule dopamine, generally found in mussel adhesive proteins [32]. The self-polymerization ability of dopamine under basic conditions generates a network with plenty of functional groups such as imines, o-hydroxyl, and amines, which endues the possibility of interacting with the analytes [32–35]. The FCDs-PDA blends obtained in the present study are to be fluorescent and exhibit a quenching signal in the presence of IBF. Moreover, the same material showed higher efficacy for the adsorptive removal of IBF from an aqueous environment. The current approach significantly simplifies the synthetic procedure for molecular imprinting of FCDs and proves to be an effective tool for removing IBF in diverse complex matrices.
Table S1 contains a list of chemicals and materials required for synthesis. Table S2 gives detailed information on the tools used to characterize and evaluate PIP-FCDs.

Rice straw was collected from a nearby paddy field in Pinjore, Haryana, India. Distilled water was used to eliminate any remaining surface contaminants on it. The rice straw was left to soak in distilled water for approximately 6 h. Then, it was dried (60 °C for 1 h) and crushed for application (as a carbon source) in pursuit of the production of FCDs using a hydrothermal approach [36]. For 15 min, 200 mg of rice straw was introduced into 40 mL of distilled water while continuously stiring at 500 rpm on a magnetic stirring device. The resulting product was put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling to ambient temperature, the resultant brown yellowish suspension was removed from the autoclave and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm (5 °C) for 10 min. A 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore, USA) was employed to filter the supernatant to remove any larger particles. Under UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, the resultant FCDs displayed blue luminescence (Fig. S1). The as-prepared FCD solution was then stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polydopamine imprinted fluorescent carbon dots (PIP-FCDs) and polydopamine non-imprinted carbon dots (PNIP-FCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The PIP-FCDs were produced via self-polymerization of dopamine on the surface of FCDs. Firstly, 5 mL of as dispersed FCDs were mixed with 10 mL buffer (potassium dihydrogen phosphate/sodium hydroxide buffer; 10 mM; pH = 8) and ethanol (20 mL). The mixture was then sonicated (10 min). Thereafter, IBF (20 mg) and dopamine (80 mg) were introduced to the mixture with a continous stirring (700 rpm) at room temperature for 12 h. The obtained product was washed with distilled water, followed by methanol-acetic acid (70:30; v/v) to remove the IBF. The applied mixture of methanol-acetic acid was examined by a UV spectrophotometer to ensure the removal of the template. The total eradication of the template was verified by the lack of an IBF signal in the UV spectrum (Fig. S2). Prior to being characterized, the obtained polymer was dried and kept in a vacuum desiccator. Similarly, PNIP-FCDs were prepared using an identical method, excluding the use of an IBF template as a control. PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs were then dried under N2 gas and stored (4 °C).

Competitive analogs (specifically ketoprofen (KETO), aspirin (ASP), levofloxacin (LEVO), and norfloxacin (NOR) were employed to compete alongside the IBF for the imprinted sites to assess the selectivity of the PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs (Table S4). The present investigation introduced 10 mg of PIP-FCDs or PNIP-FCDs into 50 mL of solution with 30 mg L−1 IBF, KET, ASP, LEVO, and NOR. The polymers were separated after 30 min of stirring, followed by UV investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence studies and real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The spectrofluorimetric performance of PIP-FCDs was evaluated by mixing 100 μL of a 10.00 mM IBF stock solution with a 2.0 mL stock suspension of PIP-FCDs in distilled water, together with buffer solution (500 μL; pH 7). Following a 10-min incubation period, the emission intensity of the resulting solution was measured at 510 nm by exciting the suspension at 415 nm and denoted as “F.” Likewise, the emission intensity of a control sample—one without IBF—was taken and denoted as “F0.” The change in fluorescence intensity was determined using the formula ΔF (ΔF = F0-F). A spectrofluorimetric titration was initiated to establish the quantification parameters by gradually adding 20 μL of a 10.0 mM IBF solution in methanol to a 2.00 mL stock solution of PIP-FCDs until a stable plateau was established. Water samples taken from a 7.5 MLD sewage treatment facility in Dhanas, Punjab, as well as laboratory tap water, were included in the study. Glass bottles were used to collect the water samples, which were refrigerated to 4 °C until analysis. The aqueous samples were filtered via filter paper (Whatman Grade 1) to remove any larger unwanted suspended particles, followed by a membrane filter (pore size of 0.22 μm). In order to demonstrate the usefulness of the proposed material, the collected samples were first examined by the PIP-FCDs probe for the possibility of IBF presence through spectrofluorimetry. However, IBF was not present in the aqueous samples. Thereafter, both samples, i.e., sewage as well as tap water were spiked with IBF stock solution at a series of concentrations of 1, 5, and 10 ng mL−1. The resulting mixture was gently shaken at room temperature. Finally, solutions from all of the samples were collected and analyzed by utilizing the PIP-FCDs (or PNIP-FCDs). Pharmaceutical formulations were obtained from the market of Chandigarh (India) in two combinations; one with 200 mg IBF and another with 400 mg IBF. Five tablets were crushed down and homogenized into a powder for each evaluation. One hundred milligrams of the pulverized tablets were dissolved in the methanol, and the resulting solution was then passed via a membrane filter (pore size of 0.22 μm) to obtain a clear filtrate. In accordance with the requirements, the solution was subsequently diluted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthetic platform construction of the PIP-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal treatment of rice straw at 180 °C for 12 h resulted in the formation of FCDs, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Furthermore, the synthetic process comprises the assembly of FCDs (surface) and dopamine (functional monomer/crosslinker) in the presence of IBF (template) to initiate the development of PIP-FCDs. It is proposed that both covalent oxidative polymerization and non-covalent self-assembly may allow PDA to adhere to the surface [37]. Additionally, PDA has reactivity due to reactive functional groups (hydroxyl and amine), which can promote a series of biochemical reactions for surface modification. In the synthesized PIP-FCDs, it seems that dopamine could bind to the FCDs through catechol via hydrogen bonding and/or π-π stacking (hydrophobic interactions). However, contributions from covalent bonding should not be excluded entirely [38]. After that, the reactants self-polymerized rapidly in an alkaline environment (pH = 8) and combined FCDs with polydopamine layer. Through non-covalent interactions, IBF molecules integrate into the cross-linked polymeric network via the amine and hydroxyl groups on PDA chains. Dopamine with several amino, hydroxyl, and phenyl groups appears to interact with IBF through multiple hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. Imprinted cavities are created in the PIP-FCDs when IBF is removed (Scheme 1). The discussed synthetic method avoided the lengthy processes traditionally applied to fabricate FCDs-based MIPs. Furthermore, the synthesis process is efficient, convenient, and green due to FCDs (biomass-derived) and dopamine (monomer/cross-linker) during the imprinting process.

To verify and support the nature of interactions, DFT calculations were done using Gaussian 16 software. Becke's three parameter hybrid correlation functional B3LYP was employed for geometry optimization with 6–31 + G (d,p) on the C, H, N, and O basis set. By taking the monomeric unit of PDA as a demonstrative of the interactive site with IBF units, we observed that the carboxylate group of IBF forms hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups of PDA. The bond lengths for interaction between the carboxylate group and oxygen atom [(C=O----H-O) and (O-H---OH)] are found to be 1.9332 Å and 1.8149 Å, respectively (Fig. S3). Similarly, the hydroxyl group in IBF and amine groups (NH2) in PDA also form hydrogen bonds (N-H-----O-H) with bond lengths of 2.1325 Å. The above hydrogen bond lengths fall in the desired range, ultimately supporting the interaction mechanism.

The FTIR spectra exhibited characteristic bands for the functional groups on the FCDs and PIP-FCDs surface. For FCDs, the prominent adsorption peaks can be assigned as the OH stretch at 3251 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1701 cm−1, OH bending vibration at 1421 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1121 cm−1 and C-O-C stretch at 1044 cm−1 (Fig. 1a). Additionally, FT-IR spectra of PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs almost mimic each other's spectrum, suggesting no significant change in functional groups; however, only imprints with a specific cavity for IBF are formed in the case of PIP-FCDs. PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs show characteristic bands of catechol and amine groups that confirm the assembly of the PDA layer on the FCDs (Fig. 1a). The bands at 3408 cm−1 (NH stretch), 1616 cm−1 (superposition of NH bending and phenylic CC stretch), and aromatic CH bending (1383 cm−1 for PIP-FCDs and 1393 cm−1 for PNIP-FCDs) confirms the existence of PDA with the FCDs.

The textural characteristics of PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs were investigated using the nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherm. The specific area of the surface and distribution of pore sizes were calculated using the BET model and the BJH method, respectively. Figs. 1b and c show the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of the materials. The mesoporous structure of PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs is supported by the found adsorption-desorption isotherms, which, by IUPAC standards, belong to the Type II branch (H3 hysteresis loop). The distinctive feature of the Type H3 loop coincides the lower limit of the desorption branch which is normally located at the cavitation-induced p/p°. The surface area of PIP-FCDs was 184.09 m2 g−1, greater in contrast to its counterpart PNIP-FCDs (131.57 m2 g−1). These findings show that PIP-FCDs had increased surface area because of imprints and cavities. Furthermore, the pore volume and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) average pore diameter values are 0.094 cm3 g−1 and 3.63 nm, and 0.065 cm3 g−1 and 2.35 for the PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs, respectively. The pore size distribution analysis further confirms that all the analyzed materials contain mesopores (2–13nm pore diameter) in their structures.

The thermal stability of each material was evaluated using TGA from 25 to 1000 °C (Fig. 1d and e). When the temperature is increased from room temperature to around 150 °C for FCDs, the TGA trend reveals entire mass loss as a result of the expulsion of water molecules and the degradation of organic functional groups (Fig. 1d). In contrast, PIP-FCDs demonstrated their first mass loss of water molecules (10.8 %) under 200 °C. The subsequent mass loss (20.7 %) at 200–550 °C was due to the removal of chemisorbed water. The third and final mass loss of 42.6 % at 550–1000 °C was due to the degredation of the PDA layer and molecular chain. These outcomes demonstrated that the PDA was effectively fabricated onto the surface of FCDs. Overall, PIP-FCDs displayed reasonably good thermal stability as they lost approximately 74.4 % of their mass at temperatures between room temperature and 1000 °C (Fig. 1e). Additionally, as the material being studied had no pre-treatments prior to the TGA, the quantity of moisture and other contaminants retained on their surfaces may have impacted the TGA result, leading to biases.

XPS was used to further identify the functional groups and analyze the surface chemistry of PIP-FCDs. C, N, and O are the three main components of PIP-FCDs, as seen in Fig. 2a. The survey spectrum exhibits intense signals corresponding to C 1 s at 285.57 eV, N 1 s at 399.05 eV and O 1 s at 530.09 eV The high-resolution spectra of carbon (Fig. 2b) revealed peaks at 284.48, 285.77, and 286.15 eV, conforming to C − C/CC, C − OH, and CO groups. In nitrogen high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2c), two peaks were displayed at 399.37 and 401.31 eV, attributed to C − N − C and N − H, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of oxygen revealed three peaks at 531.18, 532.48, and 534.24 eV (Fig. 2d), assigned to the CO, C − O, and O=C − O groups, respectively. The above XPS studies indicated that the PIP-FCDs have multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

In order to look at the morphology and size of prepared FCDs, PIP-FCDs, and PNIP-FCDs, HR-TEM images were recorded. FCDs are uniform and have a spherical shape with an average diameter of 4–9 nm (Fig. 3a and b). FCDs feature evenly distributed spherical particles that lack visible aggregation and are well isolated from one another. The PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs had similar spherical morphology with chain-like nodular structure (Fig. 3c – f). After the PDA was fabricated onto the FCDs surface, the PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs exhibited a rough polymeric surface with a size of 70–100nm. The shell thickness of the PIP-FCDs layer was about 33–45 nm. This imprinting layer thickness would work well for mass transfer activities between the solution and the PIP-FCD surface. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was used to gather further information on the FCDs, PIP-FCDs, and PNIP-FCDs phase characteristics. The SAED patterns of FCDs, PIP-FCDs, and PNIP-FCDs display diffused rings demonstrating the amorphous phase of these materials (Fig. 3g – i). EDS analysis was employed to characterize the elemental composition of FCDs, PNIP-FCDs, and PIP-FCDS (Fig. 3j – l). In line with our general expectations, carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) were dispersed in the analyzed materials. In the EDS layered image of FCDs, only C and O are present, as shown in the map data in Fig. 3j. However, after fabrication of the PDA on the FCDs, the EDS layered image shows the appearance of N peak, which corresponds to the amino group in PDA (Fig. 3k and l). Such findings show that PIP with the FCDs are formed quite effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the adsorption process variables for enhancing extraction efficiency

ce:section.ce:para:
Two isotherm models, Freundlich and Langmuir, have been examined towards the adsorptive removal of IBF by PIP-FCDS and PNIP-FCDs to investigate the link among the adsorbed amount of IBF and corresponding equilibrium concentration. Langmuir and Freundlich's isotherm model fits (linear and non-linear modes) are plotted in Fig. S4 – S6. The summary of each isotherm parameter at optimized conditions may be seen in Table 1 . Comparing experimental data points (
R 2
and
χ 2
values) for IBF, the Langmuir model (
R 2
0.9991;
χ 2
0.13 for PIP-FCDs and
R 2
0.9969 and
χ 2
0.14 for PNIP-FCDs) fits well than the Freundlich model (
R 2
0.8308;
χ 2
41.76 for PIP-FCDs and
R 2
0.9257 and
χ 2
5.42 for PNIP-FCDs) (Table 1). These results indicate that most of the binding sites on the polymers were independent and identical. It implies that the adsorption process was carried out on a single layer and uniform surface, and all the binding energies were uniform. The Langmuir
q m
values were found to be 209.8 mg g−1 for PIP-FCDs and 124.2 mg g−1 for PNIP-FCDs. Their corresponding RL values were 0.008 and 0.125, respectively. The value of RL for PIP-FCDs was considerably lower than for PNIP-FCDs, which suggests that the binding affinity of IBF towards the PIP-FCDs was more favorable. Also, the n value for IBF over PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs were computed as 5.5 and 3.5, respectively, indicating the easy uptake of IBF molecules from the solution. These results might be accounted by the possibility that the imprinting procedure in the PIP-FCDs could create specific sites for recognition with a stronger affinity for IBF. On the other hand, in PNIP-FCDs, as a result of the absence of recognition sites, non-specific interactions predominated in the IBF adsorption process. Thus, the adsorption capacity of PNIP-FCDs towards IBF was low.

The adsorption equilibrium time for both PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs was 30 min. It the strong time dependence of the adsorption capacities of PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs towards IBF. To investigate the mechanism of the adsorption process of PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs for IBF, three kinetic models (PSO, PFO, and IPD) were applied to analyze the data.

The linear and non-linear fitted curves of kinetic models are plotted in Fig. S7 – S10. As illustrated in Table 2 , the PSO model is more significant concerning their high R2 and low
χ 2
values. For PIP-FCDs, the R2 and
χ 2
values for PFO and PSO are 0.9278 and 4.85, 0.9971 and 0.12, respectively. For PNIP-FCDs, the R2 and
χ 2
values for PFO and PSO are 0.9688 and 2.11; 0.9934 and 0.23, respectively. The apparent prevalence of chemisorption over physisorption in the adsorption of IBF upon the polymers may be shown by the better fitting of the PSO model. These results suggest that the surface adsorption processes being carried out in the imprinted cavities on the surfaces of the PIP-FCDs may constitute the driving force for the rate-limiting step.

The related parameters for the IPD model fitting are shown in Table 2 and presented in Fig. S10. The IPD curves for PIP-FCD and PNIP-FCD were multi-linear, indicating that the adsorption phenomena involves these three key phases: (i) film diffusion stage: IBF molecules quickly drift across the bulk liquid phase to the polymer surface because of the abundance of free binding sites and large IBF concentrations in the medium, (ii) intra-particle diffusion stage: in which IBF molecules diffuse through the polymer's surface to its internal cavities, and (iii) surface chemical reaction: in which interactions between the adsorbate and adsorbent occur till equilibrium is reached.

The C value for IBF was 83.44 mg g−1 on the PIP-FCDs and 38.37 mg g−1 on the PNIP-FCDs (Table 2). Higher C values imply higher rates of adsorption, particularly after the transfer of mass from the bulk of the aqueous medium to the surface of the adsorbent, which is taken into account. The KID values for the IBF over PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs were 8.12 and 6.58 mg g−1 min-1/2, respectively. These observations indicate the favorability of IBF for the IPD within the PIP-FCDs pores compared to the PNIP-FCDs. IPD is probably not the sole phase in the entire adsorption process that is rate-limiting, as seen by the linear IPD plots without passing via origin. Additionally, significant intercepts in the IPD modeling show that surface adsorption significantly contributed to the rate-controlling step [40].

Several important thermodynamic parameters including ΔG° (kJ mol−1), ΔH° (kJ mol−1), and ΔS° (kJ mol−1 K−1) (Table S5). were computed to better understand the variables regulating the adsorption of IBF onto the PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs. The ΔH° and ΔS° values were computed from the slope and offset of the ln
K C
versus 1/T curve (Fig. S11). As a result, the negative ΔG° values (for IBF) at each of the investigated temperature show that the adsorption process is spontaneous and feasible [41]. The IBF adsorption method tends to be spontaneous at low temperatures, as demonstrated by the rise in magnitude of ΔG° with a decrease in temperature. The exothermic character of IBF adsorption by the polymers is further supported by the negative ΔH° values. It appears that the driving forces for IBF adsorption are sufficient for getting beyond the potential barrier [42]. The IBF molecules adsorb at the solid-liquid interface in an ordered way, as shown by the negative ΔS° values [43]. As a result, it is believed that the adsorption of IBF onto PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs is an enthalpy-driven process. It can be seen from the data in Table S5 that the ΔG° value of IBF on the PIP-FCDs is smaller than that on the PNIP-FCDs. These results demonstrate that the PIP-FCDs can extract IBF more effectively than PNIP-FCDs.

The molecular recognition ability of the PIP-FCDS was dependent on specific interactions between IBF and PIP-FCDS. To investigate the specific recognition of PIP-FCDS, a selective binding experiment was conducted using ASP, KET, NOR, and LEVO as competitive substances. The adsorption experiment for each substrate was carried out under the same conditions, and all the substrates were at the same concentration of 30 mg L−1.

As illustrated in Table 3 , the adsorption capacity of IBF on PIP-FCDs was higher than the other reference compounds. It indicates that the PIP-FCDs have a higher affinity for IBF than the other related compounds. Although the other competitive substances (ASP, KET, NOR, and LEVO) contain carboxyl groups capable of forming hydrogen bonding with PIP-FCDs, they still exhibited a higher binding capacity for IBF. This could be explained as due to the imprinting of the IBF molecules in the matrix of PIP-FCDs during the imprinting process. The imprinted cavities complementary to the size and shape of the template were established after the removal of the template. In contrast, the adsorption capacity of PNIP-FCDs for IBF and other analogues was almost the same. PNIP-FCDs, have no imprinted cavities and sites generated through polymerization, so PNIP-FCDs adsorbed these compounds through non-specific interactions.

In addition, the IF and Kd of the IBF and competitive analogues on PIP-FCDS and PNIP-FCDs were calculated for selectivity evaluation, as summarised in Table 3. Good imprinting effects were indicated by higher IFs for IBF (4.65) than for the other analogues (Table 3). The Kd of PIP-FCDs value for IBF (47.8 L g−1) was greater than that of PNIP-FCDs. PIP-FCDs demonstrated that the distribution of imprinted cavities/recognition sites was ordered in a pre-determined orientation. The α values of the PIP-FCDs were also greater (261.0–770.9) than the corresponding α values of the PNIP-FCDs (1.53–3.65) for IBF, indicating PIP-FCDs has better selectivity for IBF than the PNIP-FCDs (Table S6). Additionally, the calculated β values for IBF were significantly higher than the unity (170.3–211.2), indicating that the PIP-FCDs exhibited higher selectivity towards IBF in comparison to PNIP-FCDs (Table S6). Moreover, it was found that the binding capacity and IF values of ASP and KET are somewhat higher than the NOR and LEVO. A possible explanation is due to the resemblance of the structure of ASP and KET with IBF. All these findings verified that the PIP-FCDs had excellent recognition ability and higher selectivity for IBF than PNIP-FCDs. This leads to the selective adsorption of IBF than the other competitive substances including structural analogues.

The 3D fluorescence spectrum of the PIP-FCDs at various excitation wavelengths from 300 to 700 nm was studied at a data interval of 5.0 nm. As shown in a snapshot (Fig. 5a), an intense emission peak was detected centered at 510 nm at an excitation wavelength of 415 nm and this wavelength was chosen for more investigations. Fig. 5b further illustrates the enhancement of fluorescent intensity after the surface modification of FCDs, verifying that the fluorescent intensity of PIP-FCDs was much stronger than that of FCDs. Even though FCDs may emit fluorescent emissions, the fluorescence intensity is not often substantial because of their amorphous form and the energy relaxation that takes place in the radiationless pathway. As a result, it is possible to enhance fluorescence emission by reducing the likelihood of the radiationless pathway by stabilizing the surface of FCDs with certain substances in a nodular-like form. In a strong alkaline media, as was noted in the introduction, dopamine can quickly produce PDA, which may adhere to the surfaces of numerous materials including FCDs. Due to these factors, the produced PDA is anticipated to bind FCDs via strong hydrogen bonding and/or π-π stacking (hydrophobic interactions). Thus, the projected fluorescence increase from this technique may offer a sophisticated sensing probe for recognizing IBF. Furthermore, a comparison between PIP-FCDs (with and without IBF) and the PNIP-FCDs was investigated. It was concluded that the PNIP-FCDs show better fluorescence emission while PIP-FCDs with IBF display a weak fluorescent signal. However, the emission intensity of PIP-FCDs was recovered following the removal of IBF and was equal to that observed for PNIP-FCDs (Fig. 5b).

The intereactions between IBF and PIP-FCDs via hydrogen bonding lead to the fluorescence quenching of the sensing probe. Fluorescence quenching could be caused by photo-induced electron transfer from excited state FCDs to IBF. The radiative recombination of the electron-hole pairs is considered to be the basis of the fluorescence in PIP-FCDs. An emission peak is produced at 510 nm in absence of IBF. In the imprinting cavities of PIP-FCDs, the amine and hydroxyl groups can interact with the IBF through H-bonding and hydrophobic interactions after the inclusion of IBF. The UV absorption band gaps of both IBF as well as PIP-FCDs are adjacent to each other (Fig. 5c). The electrons in the conduction band of excited PIP-FCDs can transfer to lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of IBF leading to the fluorescence quenching. Additionally, as the fluorescence emission spectrum of PIP-FCDs does not exhibit any spectral overlap with the absorption spectrum of IBF, the electron transfer involving PIP-FCDs and IBF was the most likely explanation for the fluorescence quenching (Fig. 5d).

After conducting a time-dependent fluorescence emission investigation, the stability of the synthesized PIP-FCDs was evaluated, and no notable shift in the PIP-FCDs emission response over 100 min was discovered (Fig. 5e). Additionally, after a month, the fluorescence intensity was repeatedly detected and recorded. The intensity remained at 95.6 % of the original intensity, indicating the fluorescent sensor has satisfactory storage stability. The obtained experimental results are presented in Fig. 5f (days 1 through 30). The emission intensity measured for IBF after every 10-day cycle over a month of investigation were: 99.9 % (n = 1), 99.1 % (n = 10), 98.4 % (n = 20), and 95.6 % (n = 30). The corresponding %RSD values were 2.3 %, 1.8 %, 1.5 %, and 1.5 %, respectively. Due to the highly resilient cross-linked polymer network of PIP-FCDs probe, there is minimal fluctuation in fluorescence intensity over the course of a month, demonstrating both the physico-chemical stability of the PIP-FCDs probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition performance and sensitivity of PIP-FCDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the synthesized PIP-FCDs fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of IBF was assessed. The recognition phenomenon for both PIP-FCDs and PNIP-FCDs was assessed upon the addition of different concentrations (0–200 μL) of IBF. As soon as the quantity of IBF was increased, the emission intensities dropped (Fig. 6a). However, at the same quantity of IBF, there was no apparent drop in the emission intensity of the PNIP-FCDs (Fig. 6b). The comparison of the figures made it abundantly evident that PIP-FCDs experience a considerably more significant decline in fluorescence intensity than PNIP-FCDs. This suggests that the development of particular recognition sites with specified selectivity for IBF by the PIP-FCDs might significantly increase the quenching efficiency and, hence, increase the spectrum sensitivity to IBF.

The quenching constant (Ksv) for IBF was obtained with the help of the Stern–Volmer plots and fluorescence titration. The data was studies by applying the Stern-Volmer equation,
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
C
. F0 (without IBF) and F (with IBF) are the fluorescence intensities and [C] is the concentration of the IBF. The Stern-Volmer plots for IBF have been demonstrated to follow a non-linear fitting (Fig. 6c). As the Stern-Volmer plot for IBF shows a positive divergence from linearity, the quenching process may be static or a combination of static and dynamic. Thus, an exponential quenching equation F0/F = A·eK[C] + B was applied to the non-linear Stern–Volmer curve. A, B, and K are constants. Constants A and K together make up the quenching constant for non-linear curves. The calculated non-linear KSV for IBF was estimated as 3.90 × 102 μM.

The limit of detection (LOD) of 1.58 × 10−5 μM was obtained using the fluorescence intensity versus concentration plot (Fig. 6d) using the formula 3ϭ/k, where ϭ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and k is the slope of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of the PIP-FCDs in practice
ce:section.ce:para: Waste and tap water samples were spiked with IBF at a concentration of 48.47, 96.95, and 145.42 nM. The satisfactory recoveries were obtained for each sample (Table 4 ). The recoveries were 97.65–98.81 % (RSD %: 1.31–4.17) and 98.23–99.41 % (RSD %: 1.15–3.68) for sewage water and tap water samples, respectively. Furthermore, analysis of two different pharmaceutical formulations for IBF presence was done by PIP-FCDs, and recoveries were obtained for each sample (Table 4). The recoveries were 94.49–97.29 % (RSD %: 1.78–3.14) for Tablet 1 and 94.57–97.29 % (RSD %: 1.79–3.62) for Tablet 2. These findings showed that the suggested sensory probe may easily detect IBF in actual samples.

Manufacturing costs were computed to evaluate the PIP-FCDs' financial advantages. Assuming that rice straw is a common agricultural waste, hence their prices were not considered. In order to achieve this, the following price ranges of the chemical reagents utilized to synthesize the PIP-FCDs were taken into account: Dopamine (USD 4.2 × 103 ton−1), IBF (USD 9.61 × 102 ton−1), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (USD 1.32 × 103 ton−1), NaOH (USD 2.88 × 102 ton−1), ethanol (USD 4.2 × 102 ton−1), MeOH (USD 2.52 × 102 ton−1), and CH3COOH (USD 0.48 × 102 ton−1). The calculation was based on the cost incurred by the raw material, distilled water, chemicals (dopamine, IBF, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, NaOH, ethanol, CH3COOH, and MeOH), and power consumption (including magnetic stirring, centrifugation, autoclave synthesis, sonication, and drying). The tentative production cost per metric ton of the PIP-FCDs was calculated to be USD 1362.99 ton−1 (Table S7). The PIP-FCDs may be economically viable options for real-world applications due to their affordable cost.

In order to successfully identify and remove IBF in sewage and tap water samples, a new molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor using FCDs and PDA was designed. This sensor performs very well. Dopamine, which is self-polymerized around the FCDs as a part of the imprinting method in an alkaline solution, greatly simplifies the imprinting procedure and provided hydrophilic and biocompatible MIP materials. The LOD of PIP-FCDs towards IBF was estimated to be 1.58 × 10−5 μM. PIP-FCDs have outstanding adsorption capacity, quick adsorption rate, and high IBF removal effectiveness. It is also important to note that the present approach for detecting IBF does not require any elaborate pre-treatment steps. The cost estimate suggested that the PIP-FCDs (USD 1362.99 ton−1) contribute to the economic feasibility of the sorptive extraction methods and shows potential to be scaled-up for commercialization. The PIP-FCDs can, therefore, operate as exceptional bifunctional materials for the selective detection and removal of IBF in complex matrices by combining the rigidifying-induced fluorescence amplification and the adsorption-induced preconcentration effect. This work offers an innovative viewpoint on how PIP-FCDs are designed for the simultaneous detection and removal of IBF under simulated real-world conditions. Thus, the overall results of this study demonstrated that the as-synthesized PIP-FCDs can serve as a sensory cum adsorbent substance for the IBF in diverse media.

Irshad Mohiuddin: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Raghubir Singh: Investigation, Resources, Software, Supervision. Varinder Kaur: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge UGC-D. S. Kothari Post-Doctoral Fellowship scheme (No. F.4-2/2006 (BSR)/CH/20-21/0257) for providing financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.131765.
